title: Character N-grams in FastText
concept: The word 'apple' is split into n-grams like '<ap', 'app', 'ppl', 'ple>', representing prefixes and suffixes explicitly.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model splits words into smaller letter chunks (n-grams) to understand them?
exercises.explain: FastText is famous for using subword information (character n-grams) instead of treating words as single units.

cards.title: Words in Pieces
cards.body: What happens when an AI meets a word it has never seen before, like 'super-happy'? Standard AIs get confused and give up. But FastText is different—it chops words into tiny letter-pieces so it can guess the meaning of brand new words!

cards.title: What is an N-gram?
cards.body: A character n-gram is a small chunk of a word made of 'n' letters in a row. FastText adds special symbols '<' and '>' to mark the start and end of a word, helping it recognize prefixes and suffixes.

cards.title: The Lego Analogy
cards.body: Imagine breaking a word down into building blocks like Lego bricks. Instead of just looking at the whole toy castle, FastText looks at all the tiny connecting bricks (like 3-letter combinations). Even if you have never seen this specific castle before, you recognize the bricks it is made of!

cards.title: The Word 'cat' in Pieces
cards.body: Let's look at 3-grams (chunks of 3 letters) for the word 'cat'. FastText adds start and end symbols, making it '<cat>'. The 3-grams are: '<ca', 'cat', and 'at>'. Notice how '<ca' shows it starts the word, and 'at>' shows it ends it!

cards.title: Not Just Extra Work!
cards.body: People think that breaking words into tiny letter pieces makes the AI slower and less accurate. In fact, it makes FastText much smarter at handling typos, slang, and brand-new words because it recognizes the root parts of those words!

cards.title: Word2Vec vs. FastText
cards.body: See how classic Word2Vec sees a word as one giant block, while FastText breaks it down to capture the prefix and suffix!

left.text: Sees 'apple' as one single, indivisible word block. Cannot understand parts of it.

right.text: Sees '<ap', 'app', 'ppl', 'ple>'. Easily recognizes prefixes and suffixes.

exercises.prompt: Why does FastText add '<' and '>' symbols to the word 'apple' before splitting it?

options.text: To show that the word is in uppercase.
To mark the start and end boundaries of the word.
To delete the word from the model.

exercises.explain: The angle brackets '<' and '>' are used to explicitly mark where a word starts and where it ends.

exercises.prompt: True or False: FastText only splits words into groups of exactly 2 letters.
exercises.explain: FastText creates n-grams of different lengths (usually from 3 to 6 characters) to capture various word parts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about how FastText reads words.
exercises.sentence: FastText splits words into smaller pieces called character _______ to understand their structure.
exercises.explain: FastText chops words into character n-grams, which are small chunks of letters, to learn subword information.

exercises.prompt: If a user types a brand new word with a typo, which AI model is better at guessing its meaning?

options.text: Word2Vec, because it only looks at prefixes.
FastText, because it uses character n-grams to understand word parts.
Both models treat all unseen words the exact same way.

exercises.explain: Traditional Word2Vec treats each word as a single entity and fails on unseen words, while FastText looks inside words using character n-grams.

exercises.prompt: How does using character n-grams help FastText understand a brand new, unseen word?

options.text: It memorizes all misspelled words in the dictionary.
It translates words to different languages automatically.
It builds meaning from familiar subword pieces like prefixes and suffixes.
It ignores words it has not seen before.

exercises.explain: By breaking words into subword n-grams, FastText can understand parts of words (like 'un-', '-ing', or '-ly') even if the exact whole word was never in its training data!
